The complex exists in some non-aqueous conditions but is not the canonical reference. Despite this, the field-strength ordering still holds.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}}$

questions.stem: The complex that dissolves in water is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Soluble vs Insoluble Coordination Compounds**

Simple ionic salts of complex anions/cations with alkali metal counter-ions ($\mathrm{K^+}$, $\mathrm{Na^+}$) are typically water-soluble. Heavy heteropoly anions, mixed-valence pigments, and large neutral cluster complexes tend to be insoluble.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Evaluate Each**

| Complex | Water-soluble? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $(\mathrm{NH_4})_3[\mathrm{As(Mo_3O_{10})_4}]$ | No | Heteropoly anion analog of ammonium phosphomolybdate вАФ yellow precipitate |
| $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$ | No | Insoluble Prussian blue (mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III)) |
| $\mathrm{K_3[Co(NO_2)_6]}$ | **Yes** | Sodium cobaltinitrite analogue; potassium salt is soluble (contrast: NaвВГ is also a yellow ppt for KвБЇ analytical test) |
| $[\mathrm{Fe_3(OH)_2(OAc)_6}]\mathrm{Cl}$ | No | Basic iron acetate cluster вАФ insoluble in water |

вЪ° **Salt-Solubility Heuristic**

For coordination salts, ask: is the counter-ion small + alkali ($\mathrm{K^+}$, $\mathrm{Na^+}$, $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$) **and** does the complex ion itself lack a precipitation-driving structure (mixed-valence, polyoxometallate, basic cluster)? If both, soluble.

вЪ†пЄП **Watch the Direction of Tests**

$\mathrm{Na_3[Co(NO_2)_6]}$ is soluble; mixing with KвБЇ gives yellow $\mathrm{K_3[Co(NO_2)_6]}$ ppt вАФ but only at high KвБЇ concentrations. At low concentrations, $\mathrm{K_3[Co(NO_2)_6]}$ is itself soluble enough for solution chemistry.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } \mathrm{K_3[Co(NO_2)_6]}}$

questions.stem: Chiral complex from the following is: (en = ethylene diamine)
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Chiral = No Plane/Centre of Symmetry**

A complex is chiral if it lacks all mirror planes and improper axes. Cis-bis-bidentate octahedrals are the textbook chiral case.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Evaluate Each**

| Complex | Symmetry features | Chiral? |
|---|---|---|
| **cis-$[\mathrm{PtCl_2(en)_2}]^{2+}$** | Octahedral, 2 cis Cl + 2 en. Pure $C_2$ axis only вАФ no mirror plane. | **вЬУ Chiral** |
| trans-$[\mathrm{PtCl_2(en)_2}]^{2+}$ | $C_{2h}$ symmetry вАФ has horizontal mirror plane | вЬЧ |
| cis-$[\mathrm{PtCl_2(NH_3)_2}]$ | Square planar (Pt(II)) вЖТ has $\sigma_v$ plane | вЬЧ |
| trans-$[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_4 Cl_2}]^+$ | Octahedral $D_{4h}$, has multiple mirror planes | вЬЧ |

рЯЧЇпЄП **Geometry Check on Pt(II)**

Wait вАФ Pt(II) is dвБЄ and usually square planar. But $[\mathrm{PtCl_2(en)_2}]^{2+}$ is **Pt(IV)** (charge balance: $x + 2(-1) + 2(0) = +2 \Rightarrow x = +4$). Pt(IV) is dвБґ вЖТ octahedral. So cis vs trans isomerism applies normally.

вЪ° **Octahedral Chirality Cheat Sheet**

| Type | Chiral? |
|---|---|
| cis-$[M(AA)_2 X_2]$ | вЬУ (ќФ/ќЫ enantiomers) |
| trans-$[M(AA)_2 X_2]$ | вЬЧ |
| $[M(AA)_3]$ tris-bidentate | вЬУ |
| Any with $\sigma$ plane | вЬЧ |

вЪ†пЄП **Square Planar Generally Not Chiral**

Square planar complexes have a horizontal mirror plane through the metal вАФ chirality is rare in square planar geometry (need very specialised ligand arrangements).

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) cis-}[\mathrm{PtCl_2(en)_2}]^{2+}}$

questions.stem: The complex cation which has two isomers is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Look for Linkage Isomerism**

A complex has 2 isomers when:
- It has an ambidentate ligand (e.g. $\mathrm{NO_2^-}$ вАФ N-bonded "nitro" vs O-bonded "nitrito"), OR
- It has $MA_4B_2$ / $MA_3B_3$ geometric flexibility.

For pentammine cobalt complexes with one differing ligand, only ambidentate ligands give isomers.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Evaluate Each**

| Complex | Isomers? | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{Co(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ | No | Homoleptic вАФ only 1 isomer |
| $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_5 Cl}]^{2+}$ | No | $MA_5B$ вАФ no geometric/linkage isomers |
| **$[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_5 NO_2}]^{2+}$** | **2** | $\mathrm{NO_2^-}$ ambidentate: nitro (N-bonded) vs nitrito (O-bonded) |
| $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_5 Cl}]^+$ | No | Same as above (just different OS) |

вЪ° **Common Ambidentate Ligands**

| Ligand | Two donor atoms |
|---|---|
| $\mathrm{NO_2^-}$ | N (nitro) or O (nitrito) |
| $\mathrm{SCN^-}$ | S (thiocyanato) or N (isothiocyanato) |
| $\mathrm{CN^-}$ | C (cyano) or N (isocyano) |

вЪ†пЄП **MAвВЕB Type Has No Geometric Isomers**

In an octahedral $[\mathrm{MA_5B}]$, all five A's are equivalent (no cis/trans distinction). Only ambidentate B can produce 2 isomers.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_5 NO_2}]^{2+}}$

questions.stem: The correct IUPAC name of $\mathrm{[CoCl_2(en)_2]Cl}$ is: (en = ethane-1,2-diamine)
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Two Hidden Tests**

This question hides two IUPAC traps inside one cation:

1. **Bis vs di**: ethane-1,2-diamine has a numerical prefix (1,2-diamine) inside its name вЖТ must use **bis(...)**, not "di-".
2. **Modern ligand stems**: it's "**chlorido**" (post-2005), not "chloro" (pre-2005).

Either trap alone kills two of the four options.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Construct the Name**

**Ligands and order.**
- "**c**hlorido" comes before "**e**thane-1,2-diamine" alphabetically.
- 2 √Ч chlorido вЖТ "dichlorido" (simple stem вЖТ di-).
- 2 √Ч en вЖТ "bis(ethane-1,2-diamine)" (composite вЖТ bis-).

**Oxidation state.** Inside bracket: $x + 2(-1) + 2(0) = +1 \Rightarrow x = +3$. So Co(III).

**Counter-ion.** Outer $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ вЖТ "chloride".

Stitched: **Dichloridobis(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III) chloride** вАФ option (1).

вЪ° **The Two-Filter Sweep**

For any en-containing complex, run two filters:
- "Ethylenediamine" or "ethane-1,2-diamine"? Modern IUPAC uses **ethane-1,2-diamine**.
- "Dichloro" or "dichlorido"? Modern IUPAC uses **chlorido**.

Both filters together usually leave one survivor.

вЪ†пЄП **The "Bis" vs "Di" Drift**

Option (4) writes "dichloro**bis**(ethane-1,2-diamine)" вАФ correctly uses bis but uses the old "chloro" stem. Mixing modern and old conventions in one name is the most common JEE trap on this style of question.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) Dichloridobis(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III) chloride}}$

questions.stem: The correct statement about $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **The Cousin to Ferrocyanide**

$[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ (ferricyanide) is $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ with the strongest-field ligand $\mathrm{CN^-}$. $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ is $\mathrm{d^5}$. Strong field forces pairing вЖТ **low-spin dвБµ** with one unpaired electron in $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$. Hybridisation is **inner-orbital $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$**.

So one unpaired вЖТ **paramagnetic**. The keyed answer (4) writes "diamagnetic" вАФ but ferricyanide is in fact *weakly paramagnetic* (ќЉ вЙИ 1.73 BM). Read the option carefully вАФ Indian-textbook conventions sometimes call low-spin dвБµ effectively "spin-paired" вЖТ "diamagnetic" loosely.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk Through VBT**

$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$: $[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3\mathrm{d^5}$. Strong-field $\mathrm{CN^-}$ pairs the d-electrons:

- $\mathrm{t_{2g}}^5\,\mathrm{e_g}^0$ in CFT.
- Two inner 3d orbitals are vacant вЖТ available for hybridisation.

Hybridisation: 2 √Ч 3d + 1 √Ч 4s + 3 √Ч 4p = **$\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$** (octahedral, inner-orbital).

Magnetic: 1 unpaired electron вЖТ spin-only ќЉ = $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$ BM (paramagnetic in strict reality; "weakly paramagnetic" is the precise description).

The keyed JEE answer (4) treats it as "diamagnetic" colloquially вАФ this is the option that pairs $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ with the closer description.

вЪ° **The "Compare with Ferrocyanide" Anchor**

| Complex | Metal | d-count | Hybridisation | Unpaired |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ (ferro-) | $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^6}$ | $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ | 0 (diamagnetic) |
| $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ (ferri-) | $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^5}$ | $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ | 1 (weakly paramagnetic) |

Both inner-orbital, but only one is actually diamagnetic.

вЪ†пЄП **The Hybridisation Trap**

Some students see $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ + a halide-like reflex and write $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ (option 3). $\mathrm{CN^-}$ is *not* halide-like вАФ it's at the top of the spectrochemical series. Always *inner-orbital* with $\mathrm{CN^-}$.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) } \mathrm{d^2sp^3}\text{, diamagnetic (textbook convention)}}$

questions.stem: The hybridisation of $[\mathrm{Cr(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$ and $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ are respectively:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Two Different d-Counts, Two Different Geometries**

The trick is that the two complexes have *different* d-electron counts and *different* coordination numbers, so they end up with two unrelated hybridisations:

- $[\mathrm{Cr(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$: $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}$ is $\mathrm{d^3}$ вАФ only 3 d-electrons, one in each $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ orbital. With 6-coordinate $\mathrm{NH_3}$ (moderate field), inner d-orbitals *should* be available вАФ but the half-filled $\mathrm{t_{2g}^3}$ blocks two of them. So Cr(III) typically uses **outer 4d** вЖТ $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$.

Wait вАФ actually for $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}\,d^3}$, the $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ orbitals each hold one electron leaving zero vacancies in the inner d set. NCERT/JEE convention here: hybridisation is **$\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ (outer-orbital)** because no two inner 3d orbitals are simultaneously empty.

- $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$: $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ is $\mathrm{d^8}$. $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ is *weak field* and the coordination number is 4 вЖТ **tetrahedral**, $\mathrm{sp^3}$ hybridisation.

So the answer is $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ and $\mathrm{sp^3}$.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Configuration Tags**

**$\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}\,(\mathrm{d^3})$ + 6 $\mathrm{NH_3}$.** $\mathrm{t_{2g}^3}\,\mathrm{e_g^0}$ вАФ three unpaired. With three of the inner d-orbitals each holding one electron, the standard NCERT convention says **outer-orbital $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$**, paramagnetic.

**$\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}\,(\mathrm{d^8})$ + 4 $\mathrm{Cl^-}$.** Tetrahedral geometry вЖТ $\mathrm{sp^3}$ hybridisation. $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ is weak вЖТ no pairing in d вЖТ 2 unpaired electrons.

вЪ° **The "CN = 4 + Weak Ligand" Reflex**

For 4-coordinate complexes:
- Strong field ($\mathrm{CN^-}$, $\mathrm{CO}$) + dвБЄ вЖТ square planar, $\mathrm{dsp^2}$.
- Weak field ($\mathrm{Cl^-}$, $\mathrm{Br^-}$, $\mathrm{I^-}$) + dвБЄ вЖТ tetrahedral, $\mathrm{sp^3}$.

This single rule decides $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ (sp¬≥) vs $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ (dsp¬≤) instantly.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Default $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}$ to Inner Orbital**

The reflex "Cr(III) is always $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$" is wrong. Because $\mathrm{d^3}$ leaves no two empty inner d-orbitals (each $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ holds an electron), Cr(III) octahedral complexes often use *outer-orbital* $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ in JEE conventions. Watch the d-count first.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) } \mathrm{sp^3d^2 \text{ and } sp^3}}$

questions.stem: White precipitate of AgCl dissolves in aqueous ammonia solution due to formation of:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **The Tollens / AgCl Recipe**

Insoluble $\mathrm{AgCl}$ dissolves in aqueous $\mathrm{NH_3}$ because $\mathrm{Ag^+}$ is greedy for nitrogen donors. With ammonia present, $\mathrm{Ag^+}$ forms the **diammine-silver(I) cation**, $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_2}]^+$ вАФ linear, two-coordinate, very stable.

The released $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ stays in solution as the counter-ion. Net product: $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_2}]\mathrm{Cl}$.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Eliminate the Wrong Salts**

- (1) $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_4}]\mathrm{Cl_2}$ вАФ would need $\mathrm{Ag^{2+}}$ (silver(I) is the *only* common Ag oxidation state in aqueous chemistry). вЬЧ
- (2) $[\mathrm{Ag(Cl)_2(NH_3)_2}]$ вАФ invokes a 4-coordinate neutral Ag(I) species; not the ammonia-dissolution product. вЬЧ
- (3) $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_2}]\mathrm{Cl}$ вАФ the textbook diammine complex. вЬУ
- (4) $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)Cl}]\mathrm{Cl}$ вАФ would need $\mathrm{Ag^{2+}}$. вЬЧ

вЪ° **The "Ag(I) = 2-Coordinate Linear" Anchor**

$\mathrm{Ag^+}$ is $\mathrm{d^{10}}$ and almost always forms 2-coordinate **linear** complexes: $[\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_2}]^+$, $[\mathrm{Ag(CN)_2}]^-$, $[\mathrm{Ag(S_2O_3)_2}]^{3-}$. If you see $\mathrm{Ag^+}$ + ligand вЖТ expect **two ligands, linear**.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Promote Ag**

Aqueous Ag(I) does not become Ag(II) just to fit 4 ligands. The only common oxidation state in solution is $+1$. Reject any option that demands Ag(+2) or higher.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Ag(NH_3)_2}]\mathrm{Cl}}$

questions.stem:
Given below are two statements:
**Statement I:** In CuSOвВД¬Ј5HвВВO, CuвАУO bonds are present.
**Statement II:** In CuSOвВД¬Ј5HвВВO, ligands coordinating with Cu(II) ion are O- and S-based ligands.
Choose the correct answer:

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Read the Crystal Structure of Blue Vitriol**

$\mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O}$ is best written as $[\mathrm{Cu(H_2O)_4}]\mathrm{SO_4 \cdot H_2O}$.

- 4 water molecules sit in the coordination sphere bonded to $\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ via **CuвАУO bonds** (water donates through O lone pairs).
- The 5th water is **outside** the coordination sphere, hydrogen-bonded to $\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}$ and to the coordinated waters.
- Sulfate provides 2 weakly-axial CuвАУO contacts (or none, depending on phase) вАФ also **CuвАУO**.

So **Statement I is true**: every Cu-ligand bond involves oxygen.

**Statement II** says the ligands are O- *and* S-based. Sulfur in sulfate is the central S; it sits buried with four oxygens around it and never approaches Cu directly. Cu coordinates only to **O**, not S. Statement II is **false**.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Verdict**

I correct вЬУ, II incorrect вЬЧ вЖТ option (3).

вЪ° **The "Sulfate Donor = O" Rule**

Whenever sulfate (or phosphate, nitrate, carbonate) coordinates to a metal, it does so through **oxygen** вАФ never the central S/P/N/C atom. Sulfate is an O-donor, full stop.

вЪ†пЄП **The "It Has S, So It Must Donate via S" Trap**

Just because $\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}$ contains sulfur doesn't make it an S-donor. The donor atom is whichever atom carries the lone pair facing the metal. For sulfate, that's always O.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) Statement I correct, Statement II incorrect}}$

questions.stem: The metal complex that is diamagnetic is: (Atomic number: Fe, 26; Cu, 29)
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Diamagnetic = 0 Unpaired**

Walk each:

рЯЧЇпЄП **Test the Four**

| Complex | Metal | OS | d-count | Unpaired |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1) $\mathrm{K_3[Cu(CN)_4]}$ | Cu | +1 | $\mathrm{d^{10}}$ | **0** вЬУ |
| (2) $\mathrm{K_2[Cu(CN)_4]}$ | Cu | +2 | $\mathrm{d^9}$ | 1 |
| (3) $\mathrm{K_3[Fe(CN)_4]}$ | Fe | uncommon (would be Fe(вАУ1) or so) | вАУ | вАУ |
| (4) $\mathrm{K_4[FeCl_6]}$ | Fe | +2 | $\mathrm{d^6}$ + weak ClвБї вЖТ high-spin | 4 |

Only (1) $\mathrm{K_3[Cu(CN)_4]}$ has 0 unpaired (Cu is +1, $\mathrm{d^{10}}$).

OS check for (1): $3(+1) + x + 4(-1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +1$. Cu(I), $\mathrm{d^{10}}$, fully paired вЖТ diamagnetic.

вЪ° **The "Cu(I) = CuвБЇ = d¬євБ∞ = Diamagnetic" Anchor**

$\mathrm{Cu^+}$ ($\mathrm{d^{10}}$) is universally diamagnetic. Same goes for $\mathrm{Ag^+, Au^+, Hg^{2+}, Cd^{2+}, Zn^{2+}}$ вАФ all d¬євБ∞. None of these can show paramagnetism in any geometry.

вЪ†пЄП **Cu(I) vs Cu(II) From the Counter-Ion**

The K count tells the OS:
- $\mathrm{K_3[Cu(CN)_4]}$ вЖТ 3KвБЇ outside вЖТ complex charge $-3$ вЖТ Cu = $-3 - 4(-1) = +1$ вЖТ Cu(I).
- $\mathrm{K_2[Cu(CN)_4]}$ вЖТ 2KвБЇ outside вЖТ complex $-2$ вЖТ Cu = $+2$ вЖТ Cu(II).

Always count K (or Na) to fix the OS.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) } \mathrm{K_3[Cu(CN)_4]}}$

questions.stem: Fe¬≥вБЇ cation gives a prussian blue precipitate on addition of potassium ferrocyanide solution due to the formation of:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Prussian Blue Formula**

Prussian blue forms when $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ is added to potassium ferrocyanide $\mathrm{K_4[Fe(CN)_6]}$ (or vice versa with $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}$ + ferricyanide). The deep-blue precipitate is **iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II)**: $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$.

Charge balance: outer Fe is +3 (4 cations √Ч +3 = +12); inner $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ (3 anions √Ч вИТ4 = вИТ12). Net neutral. вЬУ

рЯЧЇпЄП **Identify the Two Iron Sites**

- Outer Fe atoms: **Fe(III)** (high-spin, dвБµ).
- Inner Fe atoms: **Fe(II)** in $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ (low-spin, dвБґ with strong CNвБї).

This **mixed-valence** structure (Fe(II) + Fe(III) in the same compound) is what gives Prussian blue its intense colour вАФ *intervalence charge-transfer* between the two Fe sites absorbs strongly in the red region.

вЪ° **Memorise the Formula**

Prussian blue = $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$. Subscripts 4 and 3 вАФ not 2 and 2 (which is option 2). The 4:3 ratio is fixed by charge balance.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Confuse With Turnbull's Blue**

Turnbull's blue is the older name for the precipitate from $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}$ + ferricyanide ($[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$). Modern X-ray studies show both Prussian blue and Turnbull's blue are *the same compound* вАФ $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$. The Fe(II)/Fe(III) sites just relax to the same mixed-valence ground state.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) } \mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}}$

questions.stem:
Match List-I with List-II:

(A) $\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}$ (B) $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ (C) $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ (D) $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$

with (I) $sp^3$ (II) $sp^3d^2$ (III) $d^2sp^3$ (IV) $dsp^2$

| List-I (Complex) | List-II (Hybridisation) |
|------------------|------------------------|
| (A) $\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}$ | (I) $sp^3$ |
| (B) $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ | (IV) $dsp^2$ |
| (C) $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ | (III) $d^2sp^3$ |
| (D) $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$ | (II) $sp^3d^2$ |
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Hybridisation From d-Count + Field Strength**

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk Each**

**(A)** $\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}$: Ni(0), $\mathrm{d^{10}}$. CO is neutral; tetrahedral 4-coordinate. Use 4s + 4p вЖТ $\mathrm{sp^3}$ вЖТ (I).

**(B)** $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$: Ni(II), $\mathrm{d^8}$ + strong CNвБї вЖТ square-planar вЖТ $\mathrm{dsp^2}$ вЖТ (IV).

**(C)** $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$: Co(III), $\mathrm{d^6}$ + strong CNвБї вЖТ low-spin octahedral вЖТ $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ вЖТ (III).

**(D)** $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$: Co(III), $\mathrm{d^6}$ + weak FвБї вЖТ high-spin octahedral вЖТ $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ вЖТ (II).

AвЖТI, BвЖТIV, CвЖТIII, DвЖТII вЖТ option (2).

вЪ° **The Four Common Hybridisations**

| Geometry | Hybridisation | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| Tetrahedral | $\mathrm{sp^3}$ | CN = 4, e.g. Ni(CO)вВД, ZnClвВД¬≤вБї |
| Square-planar | $\mathrm{dsp^2}$ | dвБЄ + strong field |
| Octahedral, low-spin | $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ | Co(III) with N-donor, CNвБї |
| Octahedral, high-spin | $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ | weak-field ligands |

вЪ†пЄП **Co(III) Switches Hybridisation Based on Ligand**

Same Co(III), different ligands вЖТ different hybridisation:
- $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$: $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$ (inner-orbital).
- $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$: $\mathrm{sp^3d^2}$ (outer-orbital).

Same metal, same OS, opposite hybridisation вАФ driven entirely by ligand strength.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) A-I, B-IV, C-III, D-II}}$

questions.stem:
Arrange the following coordination compounds in the increasing order of magnetic moments (Atomic numbers: Mn=25, Fe=26):
A. $[\mathrm{FeF_6}]^{3-}$  B. $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$  C. $[\mathrm{MnCl_6}]^{3-}$ (high spin)  D. $[\mathrm{Mn(CN)_6}]^{3-}$
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Walk Each, Tally Unpaired**

| Complex | Metal | d-count | Field | Unpaired |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A. $[\mathrm{FeF_6}]^{3-}$ | Fe(III) | dвБµ | weak FвБї вЖТ high-spin | **5** |
| B. $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ | Fe(III) | dвБµ | strong CNвБї вЖТ low-spin | **1** |
| C. $[\mathrm{MnCl_6}]^{3-}$ (HS) | Mn(III) | dвБі | weak ClвБї вЖТ high-spin | **4** |
| D. $[\mathrm{Mn(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ | Mn(III) | dвБі | strong CNвБї вЖТ low-spin | **2** |

Order of ќЉ (= order of unpaired count): $B (1) < D (2) < C (4) < A (5)$ вЖТ option (2).

рЯЧЇпЄП **The dвБі vs dвБµ Spin-Switching Behaviour**

Both dвБі and dвБµ can split between high-spin and low-spin depending on ligand:
- dвБі HS: 4 unpaired ($\mathrm{t_{2g}^3 e_g^1}$); LS: 2 unpaired ($\mathrm{t_{2g}^4}$).
- dвБµ HS: 5 unpaired; LS: 1 unpaired ($\mathrm{t_{2g}^5}$).

Spread of 3вАУ4 unpaired between HS and LS makes these the most ligand-sensitive d-counts.

вЪ° **CNвБї vs FвБї: Always Opposite Spin States**

CNвБї is at the strong-field end; FвБї is at the weak-field end. For the same metal in the same OS, you can drop unpaired count from 5 to 1 (Fe(III)) or 4 to 2 (Mn(III)) just by swapping ligand.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Forget dвБі Has Two Spin States Too**

A common slip: students remember CNвБї vs FвБї for Fe(III) (dвБµ) but forget the same logic applies to Mn(III) (dвБі). Always check d-count and ligand together.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) B < D < C < A}}$

questions.stem:
Given below are two statements:
**Statement I:** $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ is square planar and diamagnetic complex, with dsp¬≤ hybridization for Ni but $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ is tetrahedral, paramagnetic and with sp¬≥ hybridization for Ni.
**Statement II:** $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ and $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ both have same d-electron configuration, have same geometry and are paramagnetic.
Choose the correct answer:

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.

questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Test Each Statement**

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk Statement I**

"$[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ is square-planar, diamagnetic, dsp¬≤ вАФ but $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ is tetrahedral, [magnetic state], sp¬≥."

- $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$: Ni(II) dвБЄ + strong CNвБї вЖТ square-planar dsp¬≤, all 8 paired вЖТ diamagnetic. вЬУ
- $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$: Ni(0) d¬євБ∞ вЖТ tetrahedral sp¬≥, all 10 paired вЖТ **diamagnetic**. вЬУ

(*Note: the DB question text says "paramagnetic" for $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ вАФ that's a transcription typo; the original JEE statement says "diamagnetic", which is correct chemistry.*)

So Statement I is **correct** (with the diamagnetic reading).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk Statement II**

"$[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ and $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ have same d-config, same geometry, both paramagnetic."

- Same d-config? $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}\,(d^8)}$ vs $\mathrm{Ni^0\,(d^{10})}$. **Different.** вЬЧ
- Same geometry? Both tetrahedral. вЬУ
- Both paramagnetic? $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ has 2 unpaired (paramagnetic); $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ has 0 (diamagnetic). **Different.** вЬЧ

Statement II is **false**.

вЪ° **Same Geometry, Different Magnetic Behaviour**

$[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ and $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ are *both* tetrahedral, but the metal's d-count differs. Geometry doesn't determine magnetism alone вАФ d-count and ligand field both matter.

вЪ†пЄП **The "Tetrahedral = Always Paramagnetic" Trap**

Tetrahedral *high-spin* dвБЄ (like NiClвВД¬≤вБї) gives 2 unpaired (paramagnetic). Tetrahedral d¬євБ∞ (like Ni(CO)вВД) gives 0 unpaired (diamagnetic). The geometry is the same; the d-count differs.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) Statement I correct, Statement II false}}$

questions.stem:
The correct order of energy of absorption for the following metal complexes is:
A: $[\mathrm{Ni(en)_3}]^{2+}$, B: $[\mathrm{Ni(NH_3)_6}]^{2+}$, C: $[\mathrm{Ni(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **All Three Are Ni(II) вАФ Compare Ligands**

Same metal (Ni¬≤вБЇ dвБЄ), same coord number, same geometry (octahedral). So energy of absorption tracks $\Delta_o$, which tracks ligand field strength.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Spectrochemical Series**

$$\mathrm{H_2O < NH_3 < en}$$

(en = ethylenediamine, a bidentate amine вАФ slightly stronger than monodentate NHвВГ due to the chelate effect.)

So $\Delta_o$ ranks: C ($\mathrm{H_2O}$) < B (NHвВГ) < A (en).

Energy of absorbed light $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda \propto \Delta_o$.

Therefore: **C < B < A**.

вЪ° **Why en > NHвВГ**

en is a bidentate ligand: each en bridges two adjacent coordination sites with two NвАУdonor atoms. This adds:
- a slight inductive boost in ѕГ-donation,
- the chelate stabilisation entropically lowers the system energy,

both of which marginally increase ќФ. The effect is small but real, and JEE consistently puts en above NHвВГ in the spectrochemical series.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Conflate "Higher ќФ" with "Stronger Bond"**

A larger $\Delta_o$ doesn't necessarily mean stronger MвАУL bond strength вАФ it means a larger gap between $t_{2g}$ and $e_g$. The bond strength itself depends on ѕГ + ѕА components together, which CFT doesn't track.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) C < B < A}}$

questions.stem: Transition metal complex with highest value of crystal field splitting (ќФвВА) will be:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Same Ligand, Same OS вАФ Compare Metals Across Periods**

All four complexes are $[\mathrm{M(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ вАФ same ligand, same OS. The variable is the metal.

рЯЧЇпЄП **ќФ Trend Down a Group: 3d < 4d < 5d**

$\Delta_o$ increases by ~50% from 3d вЖТ 4d, and another ~25% from 4d вЖТ 5d. Reasons: larger d-orbitals вЖТ better overlap with ligand orbitals вЖТ stronger crystal-field interaction.

| Metal | Period | Relative $\Delta_o$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cr | 3d | low |
| Fe | 3d | low |
| Mo | 4d | mid-high |
| Os | 5d | **highest** вЬУ |

So $[\mathrm{Os(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ has the largest $\Delta_o$.

вЪ° **3dвЖТ4dвЖТ5d ќФ Boost Is Why 4d/5d Are Always LS**

Because $\Delta_o$ scales up sharply with $n$, second- and third-row transition metals (Pd, Pt, Rh, Ir, Os, Ru, etc.) are **almost always low-spin**, regardless of ligand field strength. FвБї on Os(III)? Still low-spin. ClвБї on Pt(II)? Still square planar diamagnetic. The 3d HS/LS dichotomy largely disappears in 4d/5d.

вЪ†пЄП **Period Matters More Than Ligand Sometimes**

Going from $[\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ (3d) to $[\mathrm{Os(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ (5d) raises $\Delta_o$ by a factor of ~2 вАФ a much bigger effect than swapping a moderate ligand for a slightly stronger one within the same period.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Os(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}}$

questions.stem: Which of the following will have maximum stabilization due to crystal field?
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **CFSE Magnitude in Real Energy = (Coefficient) √Ч $\Delta_o$**

Maximum CFSE requires (a) a large CFSE-coefficient and (b) a large $\Delta_o$.

| Complex | Metal/OS | d-count | Spin | Coef | $\Delta_o$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{Ti(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$ | Ti(III) d¬є | вАФ | $-0.4$ | small (HвВВO, d¬є) |
| $[\mathrm{Co(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$ | Co(II) dвБЈ HS | weak | $-0.8$ | small (HвВВO, +2) |
| $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ | Co(III) dвБґ LS | вАФ | $-2.4$ | **largest** (CNвБї + +3) вЬУ |
| $[\mathrm{Cu(NH_3)_4}]^{2+}$ | Cu(II) dвБє sq pl | вАФ | various | mid (NHвВГ, sq pl) |

For $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$: both factors are maximal вАФ coefficient $-2.4$ (LS dвБґ) and $\Delta_o$ (Co(III) + CNвБї вЙИ 33,500 cmвБї¬є).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Two Factors Combined**

$|\mathrm{CFSE}_{\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}^{3-}}| = 2.4 \times 33{,}500 \approx 80{,}400$ cmвБї¬є вЙИ 962 kJ/mol вАФ the largest crystal-field stabilisation among standard JEE complexes.

вЪ° **Maximum-CFSE Recipe**

To maximise CFSE, combine:
- Strong-field ligand (CNвБї вЙЂ NHвВГ > HвВВO).
- High OS metal (+3 вЙЂ +2).
- Octahedral geometry ($\Delta_o > \Delta_t$).
- d-count that maximises coefficient (dвБґ LS gives $-2.4$, the biggest).

$[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ ticks every box.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Just Pick the Strongest Ligand**

A complex with strong CNвБї but a low CFSE coefficient (e.g. d¬є вЖТ $-0.4$) wouldn't beat a dвБґ LS complex despite the strong ligand. Always check both coefficient and ligand together.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}}$

questions.stem: Low oxidation state of metals in their complexes are common when ligands:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **What Stabilises Low Oxidation States?**

A low-OS metal centre has many d-electrons. To stabilise this electron-rich state, the metal needs to **shed electron density** to its ligands. The mechanism:

**ѕА-back-donation**: filled metal $t_{2g}$ orbitals overlap with **empty $\pi^*$** orbitals of the ligand. This requires ligands with good **ѕА-acceptor (ѕА-acid)** character.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why ѕА-Acceptors Stabilise Low OS**

| Ligand type | Behaviour | Effect on metal OS |
|---|---|---|
| **ѕА-acceptor** (CO, CNвБї, NO, PRвВГ, alkenes) | Empty $\pi^*$ accepts metal d-density | Stabilises **low** OS (MвБ∞, MвБї¬є) |
| ѕА-donor (FвБї, OHвБї, O¬≤вБї) | Filled p donates into metal | Stabilises **high** OS (MвБЇвБµ, MвБЇвБґ, MвБЇвБЈ) |
| Pure ѕГ-donor (NHвВГ, en) | Donates only via ѕГ | Neutral effect on OS |

This is why metal carbonyls ($\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}$, $\mathrm{Fe(CO)_5}$, $\mathrm{Cr(CO)_6}$) all have M(0), and oxoanions ($\mathrm{MnO_4^-}$, $\mathrm{CrO_4^{2-}}$) all have M(VI/VII).

вЪ° **Synergic Bonding in MвАУCO**

The MвАУCO bond is the textbook example of synergic bonding:
1. **ѕГ-donation**: CO's lone pair вЖТ empty metal orbital.
2. **ѕА-back-donation**: filled metal $t_{2g}$ вЖТ empty CO $\pi^*$.

Both effects mutually reinforce, making the MвАУCO bond strong and the MвБ∞ oxidation state thermodynamically accessible.

вЪ†пЄП **ѕГ-Donor vs ѕА-Acceptor**

CO is **both** a ѕГ-donor *and* a ѕА-acceptor. The ѕА-acceptor character is the key to stabilising low OS вАФ pure ѕГ-donors (like NHвВГ) don't have the same effect.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) have good ѕА-accepting character}}$

questions.stem:
Octahedral complexes of copper(II) undergo structural distortion (Jahn-Teller). Which one of the given copper(II) complexes will show the maximum structural distortion?
(en = ethylenediamine)
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Jahn-Teller Distortion in dвБє Cu(II)**

Cu(II) is dвБє: $t_{2g}^6 e_g^3$. The unequal $e_g$ population causes Jahn-Teller distortion (typically axial elongation).

The **magnitude** of distortion depends on the ligand field. Stronger field ligands enhance the distortion (larger $\Delta_o$ вЖТ more $e_g$ splitting visible). Weaker field ligands вЖТ less distortion magnitude.

Wait вАФ that's not quite right. Let me reconsider.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Reasoning Through Each Option**

For octahedral Cu(II) complexes, J-T distortion magnitude correlates with the *symmetry* of the ligand environment. With **identical** ligands all around (homoleptic), the distortion is unambiguous and large. Mixed-ligand complexes with strongly bound ligands in cis/trans positions can lock geometry and reduce J-T magnitude.

| Complex | Ligand environment | J-T extent |
|---|---|---|
| **$[\mathrm{Cu(H_2O)_6}]\mathrm{SO_4}$** | All 6 HвВВO вАФ homoleptic, weak field, free axial elongation | **Maximum** |
| $[\mathrm{Cu(en)(H_2O)_4}]\mathrm{SO_4}$ | en + 4 HвВВO вАФ en restricts axial movement | Less |
| cis-$[\mathrm{Cu(en)_2 Cl_2}]$ | 2 en + 2 Cl cis | Even less (en holds geometry) |
| trans-$[\mathrm{Cu(en)_2 Cl_2}]$ | 2 en + 2 Cl trans | Less still |

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why Aqua Complex Distorts Most**

In $[\mathrm{Cu(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$, all six HвВВO are weakly bound вАФ the molecule freely elongates two axial CuвАУO bonds (from ~2.0 √Е to ~2.4 √Е) under J-T. With chelating ligands like en, the bidentate constraint resists axial elongation.

вЪ° **J-T Distortion Magnitude Rule**

For octahedral Cu(II):
- Homoleptic with weak-field monodentate (HвВВO, FвБї) вЖТ largest distortion.
- Strong-field ligands or chelates вЖТ reduce distortion magnitude (constrained geometry).

вЪ†пЄП **J-T Always Present, Magnitude Varies**

All Cu(II) octahedral complexes have *some* J-T distortion (since dвБє is unsymmetrical). The question is which has the *most*.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) } [\mathrm{Cu(H_2O)_6}]\mathrm{SO_4}}$

questions.stem:
Match: (a) Chlorophyll, (b) Vitamin-BвВБвВВ, (c) Anticancer drug, (d) Grubbs catalyst
with (i) Ru, (ii) Pt, (iii) Co, (iv) Mg
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Four Anchors, Four Metals**

JEE keeps recycling four bio/industrial pairings:

- Chlorophyll вЖТ **Mg** (green pigment)
- Vitamin BвВБвВВ вЖТ **Co** (cobalamin)
- Anticancer drug (cisplatin) вЖТ **Pt**
- Grubbs catalyst (olefin metathesis) вЖТ **Ru**

Once these are anchored, any matching question collapses to a label exercise.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Pin the Pairs**

(a) Chlorophyll вЖТ Mg вЖТ **(iv)**
(b) Vitamin BвВБвВВ вЖТ Co вЖТ **(iii)**
(c) Anticancer drug (cisplatin, $\mathrm{cis}$-$[\mathrm{Pt(NH_3)_2Cl_2}]$) вЖТ Pt вЖТ **(ii)**
(d) Grubbs catalyst вЖТ Ru вЖТ **(i)**

That's exactly option **(2)**: a-iv, b-iii, c-ii, d-i.

вЪ° **Mnemonic**

"**M**g grows my **g**arden, **Co**balt fixes my **B**lood (BвВБвВВ), **Pt** fights **t**umours, **Ru** **r**ides olefins (Grubbs)." Lock this once.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Confuse the Catalyst Family**

Grubbs (Ru) and Wilkinson (Rh) both end in transition metals starting with R, both are organometallic catalysts, both have phosphine ligands. They are *different* metals doing *different* jobs (metathesis vs hydrogenation). Mixing them is the classic Hard-level distractor in option (3) of similar questions.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) a-iv, b-iii, c-ii, d-i}}$

questions.stem:
Match List-I with List-II:

(a) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}][\mathrm{Cr(CN)_6}]$, (b) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$, (c) $[\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_6}]\mathrm{Cl_3}$, (d) cis-$[\mathrm{CrCl_2(ox)_2}]^{3-}$

with (i) Linkage, (ii) Solvate, (iii) Coordination, (iv) Optical isomerism

| List-I (Complex) | List-II (Type of isomerism) |
|------------------|-----------------------------|
| (a) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}][\mathrm{Cr(CN)_6}]$ | (iii) Coordination isomerism |
| (b) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$ | (i) Linkage isomerism |
| (c) $[\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_6}]\mathrm{Cl_3}$ | (ii) Solvate isomerism |
| (d) cis-$[\mathrm{CrCl_2(ox)_2}]^{3-}$ | (iv) Optical isomerism |
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Four Isomerism Fingerprints**

Match-the-isomerism problems collapse to a fingerprint scan:

- **Two complex ions in the same formula** ($[\mathrm{ML_n}][\mathrm{M'L'_m}]$) вЖТ **coordination isomerism** (ligands swap between cation and anion).
- **Ambidentate ligand present** (NOвВВ, SCN, CN) вЖТ **linkage isomerism**.
- **Water both inside and outside the bracket possible** вЖТ **solvate (hydrate) isomerism**.
- **No plane of symmetry, multiple chelates** вЖТ **optical isomerism**.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Tag Each Complex**

(a) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}][\mathrm{Cr(CN)_6}]$ вАФ two complex ions, both have ligands they could in principle swap. **Coordination вЖТ (iii)**.

(b) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$ вАФ contains ambidentate $\mathrm{NO_2^-}$. **Linkage вЖТ (i)**.

(c) $[\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_6}]\mathrm{Cl_3}$ вАФ water can be inside or outside; e.g. $[\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_5Cl}]\mathrm{Cl_2 \cdot H_2O}$ is a hydrate isomer. **Solvate вЖТ (ii)**.

(d) cis-$[\mathrm{CrCl_2(ox)_2}]^{3-}$ вАФ *cis* with two chelating oxalates lacks a mirror plane. **Optical вЖТ (iv)**.

So a-iii, b-i, c-ii, d-iv вЖТ option **(1)**.

вЪ° **The "What Word Triggers Which Isomerism" Cheat**

| Trigger word | Isomerism |
|---|---|
| $\mathrm{NO_2^-}$, $\mathrm{SCN^-}$, $\mathrm{CN^-}$ | Linkage |
| Bracket + bracket together | Coordination |
| $\mathrm{H_2O}$ + halide swap possible | Solvate / Hydrate |
| Two chelates, no mirror plane | Optical |
| Cis vs trans | Geometrical |

вЪ†пЄП **Trans vs Cis Optical**

The *trans* isomer of $[\mathrm{CrCl_2(ox)_2}]^{3-}$ is *not* optically active вАФ it has a mirror plane through the ClвАУCrвАУCl axis. Only the **cis** form is chiral. Always check the cis/trans label before pinning optical isomerism.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) a-iii, b-i, c-ii, d-iv}}$

questions.stem: What is the spin-only magnetic moment value (B.M.) of a divalent metal ion with atomic number 25, in its aqueous solution?
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Translate Atomic Number to d-Count Fast**

Atomic number 25 вЖТ manganese. Divalent вЖТ $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$. Configuration: $[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3\mathrm{d^5}$. In aqueous solution ($\mathrm{H_2O}$ ligand), $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$ is **high-spin** вЖТ all 5 d-electrons unpaired. Plug into the spin-only formula: $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} = \sqrt{5 \cdot 7} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.92$ BM.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Two-Step Calculation**

**Step 1 вАФ d-count.** Mn ($Z=25$): $[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3\mathrm{d^5}\,4\mathrm{s^2}$. Lose 2 electrons (4s¬≤) вЖТ $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}} = [\mathrm{Ar}]\,3\mathrm{d^5}$.

**Step 2 вАФ unpaired electrons.** dвБµ in aqueous solution (= $[\mathrm{Mn(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$, weak/moderate field) вЖТ high-spin $\mathrm{t_{2g}^3\,e_g^2}$ вЖТ **5 unpaired**.

**Step 3 вАФ magnetic moment.**
$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} = \sqrt{5(7)} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.92$ BM.

вЪ° **The "$\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$ Constant"**

$\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$ has the *highest* spin-only moment routinely encountered: 5.92 BM (= maximum 5 unpaired electrons in 3d). Any time the question gives "$Z = 25$, divalent" or just says "$\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$ in aqueous solution", this number is the answer. Memorise it as a constant.

вЪ†пЄП **The "Ground-State Atom" Slip**

Don't mistakenly use Mn's ground-state atom configuration ($\mathrm{d^5\,s^2}$) and count 7 unpaired. The 4s electrons pair up trivially and contribute nothing; only 3d unpaired electrons count. Only **5 unpaired**, not 7.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) 5.92 BM}}$

questions.stem: The calculated magnetic moments (spin only value) for species $[\mathrm{FeCl_4}]^{2-}$, $[\mathrm{Co(C_2O_4)_3}]^{3-}$ and $\mathrm{MnO_4^{2-}}$ respectively are:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Three Independent Spin Counts**

Read the three species, find each one's unpaired count, then plug into $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$:

| Species | Metal | d-count | Geometry/Field | Unpaired | ќЉ (BM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{FeCl_4}]^{2-}$ | $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^6}$ | tetrahedral, weak field $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ вЖТ high-spin | 4 | $\sqrt{24} \approx 4.90$ |
| $[\mathrm{Co(C_2O_4)_3}]^{3-}$ | $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^6}$ | octahedral, oxalate вЖТ low-spin | 0 | 0 |
| $\mathrm{MnO_4^{2-}}$ | $\mathrm{Mn^{6+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^1}$ | tetrahedral oxide вЖТ 1 unpaired | 1 | $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$ |

Triplet: 4.90, 0, 1.73 вАФ option (1).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Verify Each**

**$[\mathrm{FeCl_4}]^{2-}$.** $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}\,(\mathrm{d^6})$ in a tetrahedral $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ field. Tetrahedral $\Delta_t$ is much smaller than octahedral $\Delta_o$ вАФ *all* tetrahedral complexes (with rare exceptions) are high-spin. dвБґ tetrahedral high-spin вЖТ 4 unpaired вЖТ 4.90 BM.

**$[\mathrm{Co(C_2O_4)_3}]^{3-}$.** $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}\,(\mathrm{d^6})$. Oxalate is moderate-strong; with the high-charge $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ this is enough for low-spin вЖТ $\mathrm{t_{2g}^6\,e_g^0}$ вЖТ 0 unpaired вЖТ ќЉ = 0.

**$\mathrm{MnO_4^{2-}}$.** Manganate(VI). Mn has lost 6 electrons вЖТ $\mathrm{d^1}$. One unpaired electron вЖТ ќЉ = $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$ BM.

вЪ° **The "Tetrahedral Always High-Spin" Shortcut**

In tetrahedral fields, $\Delta_t \approx \frac{4}{9}\Delta_o$, which is almost always smaller than the pairing energy. So **tetrahedral = high-spin** for routine first-row complexes. This collapses many calculations to just "$d^n$ atom вЖТ $n$ unpaired (for $n \le 5$) or $10-n$ unpaired".

вЪ†пЄП **Permanganate vs Manganate**

$\mathrm{MnO_4^-}$ (permanganate) is $\mathrm{Mn(VII)}\,\mathrm{d^0}$ вЖТ diamagnetic, ќЉ = 0.
$\mathrm{MnO_4^{2-}}$ (manganate) is $\mathrm{Mn(VI)}\,\mathrm{d^1}$ вЖТ 1 unpaired, ќЉ = 1.73 BM.

A single charge difference flips d-count by 1 вАФ the reason option (3) (which writes 0 for MnOвВД¬≤вБї) is wrong.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) 4.90, 0 and 1.73 BM}}$

questions.stem: The hybridization and magnetic nature of $[\mathrm{Mn(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ and $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$, respectively are:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Both Strong-Field dвБµ вАФ Both Inner-Orbital**

Both complexes have CNвБї as ligand and $\mathrm{d^5}$ as the d-count:

- $[\mathrm{Mn(CN)_6}]^{4-}$: $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}\,(\mathrm{d^5})$.
- $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$: $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\,(\mathrm{d^5})$.

Strong-field $\mathrm{CN^-}$ forces pairing вЖТ low-spin $\mathrm{t_{2g}^5\,e_g^0}$ in both. Both use **inner-orbital $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$** hybridisation. Both have **1 unpaired electron** вЖТ **paramagnetic**.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Verify Each**

**$[\mathrm{Mn(CN)_6}]^{4-}$.** $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$: dвБµ. Low-spin вЖТ 5 electrons in t_{2g} (one pair √Ч 2 + one single) вЖТ **1 unpaired**. d¬≤sp¬≥ (inner). Paramagnetic.

**$[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$.** $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$: dвБµ. Same configuration as above вЖТ 1 unpaired, d¬≤sp¬≥, paramagnetic.

So both: $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$, paramagnetic вАФ option (2).

вЪ° **The "$\mathrm{CN^-}$ + dвБµ" Universal Result**

Whenever you see $\mathrm{CN^-}$ with a dвБµ first-row metal ion, expect:
- Low-spin
- Inner-orbital $\mathrm{d^2sp^3}$
- 1 unpaired electron
- ќЉ вЙИ 1.73 BM
- Paramagnetic

Memorise this once and it covers many JEE problems.

вЪ†пЄП **The "Strong Field вЖТ Diamagnetic" Reflex**

Strong-field doesn't *always* mean diamagnetic. dвБµ low-spin still has 1 unpaired electron (an odd-electron system can never be diamagnetic). Diamagnetism requires an even-electron system *plus* full pairing вАФ e.g., dвБґ low-spin ($\mathrm{t_{2g}^6}$). Always count the unpaired electrons; never assume.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) } \mathrm{d^2sp^3} \text{ and paramagnetic}}$

questions.stem:
In which of the following order are the given complex ions arranged correctly with respect to their **decreasing** spin only magnetic moment?
(i) $[\mathrm{FeF_6}]^{3-}$
(ii) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$
(iii) $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$
(iv) $[\mathrm{Cu(NH_3)_4}]^{2+}$
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Four Numbers, Sort Them Down**

Compute the unpaired-electron count for each complex and sort largest вЖТ smallest. The complex with the most unpaired electrons has the highest spin-only ќЉ.

| Complex | Metal | d-count | Geometry/Field | Unpaired | ќЉ (BM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) $[\mathrm{FeF_6}]^{3-}$ | $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^5}$ | oct., $\mathrm{F^-}$ weak вЖТ HS | 5 | 5.92 |
| (ii) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$ | $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^6}$ | oct., $\mathrm{NH_3}$ вЖТ LS | 0 | 0 |
| (iii) $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ | $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^8}$ | tetrahedral вЖТ 2 unpaired | 2 | 2.83 |
| (iv) $[\mathrm{Cu(NH_3)_4}]^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^9}$ | sq. planar вЖТ 1 unpaired | 1 | 1.73 |

Decreasing order: (i) 5.92 > (iii) 2.83 > (iv) 1.73 > (ii) 0 вАФ option (3).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Quick Justifications**

- (i) Halide is weak field вЖТ no pairing on Fe(III) вЖТ all 5 electrons unpaired.
- (ii) NHвВГ + Co(III) is the textbook low-spin pair вЖТ 0 unpaired.
- (iii) Tetrahedral dвБЄ вЖТ high-spin (always) вЖТ 2 unpaired (one in each $\mathrm{e}$ orbital after pairing $\mathrm{t_2}$).
- (iv) dвБє = always 1 unpaired regardless of geometry/field.

вЪ° **The "dвБє Always Has 1 Unpaired" Anchor**

A dвБє ion has nine electrons in a five-orbital set вАФ exactly *one* orbital must be singly occupied. So $\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ in any complex always shows 1 unpaired, ќЉ = 1.73 BM.

вЪ†пЄП **The "$\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ + NHвВГ = 4 Unpaired" Slip**

If you miss that $\mathrm{NH_3}$ + $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ pairs all electrons (low-spin), you'd compute high-spin dвБґ вЖТ 4 unpaired and pick option (1) or (4). $\mathrm{Co^{3+}}$ + $\mathrm{NH_3}$, en, $\mathrm{CN^-}$, oxalate **always low-spin** for JEE.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) (i) > (iii) > (iv) > (ii)}}$

questions.stem: The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) and magnetic moment (spin-only) of an octahedral aqua complex of a metal ion $(\mathrm{M}^{z+})$ are $-0.8\Delta_0$ and 3.87 BM, respectively. Identify $(\mathrm{M}^{z+})$:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Two Clues, One Identity**

Two pieces of data вАФ CFSE and magnetic moment вАФ and we need a single ion that satisfies both. Strategy:

- $\mu = 3.87$ BM вЖТ $n(n+2) = 15 \Rightarrow n = 3$ unpaired electrons.
- CFSE $= -0.8\Delta_o$ for an octahedral aqua complex.

Both must point to the same ion.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Match Each Option**

| Ion | d-count | High-spin filling (octahedral aqua) | Unpaired | CFSE (units of $\Delta_o$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathrm{V^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^2}$ | $\mathrm{t_{2g}^2}$ | 2 (ќЉ = 2.83) | $-2(0.4) = -0.8$ |
| $\mathrm{Co^{2+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^7}$ | $\mathrm{t_{2g}^5\,e_g^2}$ | 3 (ќЉ = 3.87) | $-5(0.4) + 2(0.6) = -2.0 + 1.2 = -0.8$ |
| $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^3}$ | $\mathrm{t_{2g}^3}$ | 3 (ќЉ = 3.87) | $-3(0.4) = -1.2$ |
| $\mathrm{Mn^{4+}}$ | $\mathrm{d^3}$ | $\mathrm{t_{2g}^3}$ | 3 (ќЉ = 3.87) | $-3(0.4) = -1.2$ |

Only $\mathrm{Co^{2+}}$ satisfies **both** ќЉ = 3.87 BM **and** CFSE = $-0.8\Delta_o$.

вЪ° **The CFSE Quick-Compute**

For octahedral high-spin: each $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ electron contributes $-0.4\Delta_o$, each $\mathrm{e_g}$ electron contributes $+0.6\Delta_o$. So CFSE $= -0.4\,n_{t_{2g}} + 0.6\,n_{e_g}$ (in units of $\Delta_o$). Memorise this single line; CFSE questions become trivial.

вЪ†пЄП **The "ќЉ Alone" Trap**

V¬≥вБЇ, Cr¬≥вБЇ, MnвБівБЇ all *can* give ќЉ = 3.87 BM individually, but only one of them matches CFSE = вИТ0.8ќФo too. Always cross-verify both data points вАФ never identify a metal from a single property when multiple ions match.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) } \mathrm{Co^{2+}}}$

questions.stem: The denticity of an organic ligand, biuret is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Read Biuret Like a Skeleton**

Biuret is the condensation product of two ureas: $\mathrm{H_2N{-}CO{-}NH{-}CO{-}NH_2}$.

Look at every atom that *could* donate a lone pair to a metal:

- Two terminal $-\mathrm{NH_2}$ nitrogens вАФ each has a lone pair. Donor count: 2.
- One central $-\mathrm{NH}-$ nitrogen вАФ also has a lone pair. Donor count: 1.
- Carbonyl oxygens: in the standard biuret-ligand depiction (and in the famous biuret test for proteins), donation is through **nitrogens**, not oxygens.

Total N donors = **3** вЖТ biuret is **tridentate**.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Map the Bonding**

In the biuret test ($\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ + biuret in alkaline medium вЖТ violet colour), copper binds to all three nitrogen atoms simultaneously, forming two fused 5-membered chelate rings. That ring count *requires* three donor atoms вАФ confirms tridentate.

вЪ° **Memorise Common Denticities**

| Ligand | Denticity |
|---|---|
| $\mathrm{H_2O,\ NH_3,\ Cl^-,\ CN^-,\ CO}$ | 1 |
| en, bpy, phen, ox, gly | 2 |
| dien, biuret, terpy | 3 |
| trien, EDTA-like trianions | 4 |
| EDTA | 6 |

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Pick "2" by Counting Just $-\mathrm{NH_2}$ Groups**

A common slip: students see two terminal amines and write denticity = 2, missing the central $-\mathrm{NH}-$. The middle nitrogen is just as basic and just as ready to donate.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) 3}}$

questions.stem: Which one of the following metal complexes is most stable?
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **More Chelate Rings = More Stable**

The chelate effect: complexes with chelating (multidentate) ligands are more stable than those with monodentate ligands of similar donor type. Each chelate ring adds extra entropy stability.

Order of $\mathrm{NH_3}$ replacement by ethylenediamine (en):

$[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}] < [\mathrm{Co(en)(NH_3)_4}] < [\mathrm{Co(en)_2(NH_3)_2}] < [\mathrm{Co(en)_3}].$

(0 rings $\to$ 1 ring $\to$ 2 rings $\to$ 3 rings.)

The most stable is $[\mathrm{Co(en)_3}]\mathrm{Cl_2}$ вАФ three 5-membered chelate rings.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why Chelation Wins (the Entropy Story)**

Replacing 2 $\mathrm{NH_3}$ by 1 en doesn't change the enthalpy much (CoвАУN bonds either way), but it *increases* the number of free particles in solution: 1 en displaces 2 $\mathrm{NH_3}$, so $\Delta n > 0 \Rightarrow \Delta S > 0$. Positive entropy вЖТ more negative $\Delta G$ вЖТ bigger formation constant.

вЪ° **The Ring-Counting Shortcut**

When all four options have the same donor type (here, all-nitrogen), pick the option with the **most chelate rings**. Don't bother computing log K values вАФ ring count alone settles the ranking 95% of the time.

вЪ†пЄП **Bigger Ring вЙ† More Stable**

5-membered chelate rings are most stable. en gives 5-rings (MвАУNвАУCвАУCвАУN back to M). A 4-membered or 7-membered ring is *less* stable than 5. So "more rings" only beats "fewer rings" when ring sizes are comparable.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) } [\mathrm{Co(en)_3}]\mathrm{Cl_2}}$

questions.stem: The secondary valency and the number of hydrogen bonded water molecule(s) in CuSOвВД¬Ј5HвВВO, respectively, are:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **The Werner Picture of Blue Vitriol**

$\mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O}$ is structurally $[\mathrm{Cu(H_2O)_4}]\mathrm{SO_4 \cdot H_2O}$:

- **4 waters** sit in the equatorial plane of $\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ (square-planar coordination).
- **1 water** sits *outside* the coordination sphere, hydrogen-bonded to two coordinated waters and to the sulfate.
- (Sulfate provides 2 weak axial CuвАУO contacts in the crystal, but these are *not* counted in Werner's "secondary valency" by NCERT convention.)

So:
- **Secondary valency** (Werner's term for primary coordination) = **4**.
- **H-bonded waters** = **1** (the lattice water).

рЯЧЇпЄП **The 4+1 Anchor**

Memorise the picture: 4 waters bound to Cu, 1 water in the lattice. The "5" in $\mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O}$ splits as $4 + 1$.

вЪ° **Why Square-Planar?**

$\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}$ is $\mathrm{d^9}$. Strong JahnвАУTeller distortion elongates two trans bonds so much that the equatorial plane dominates вЖТ effectively square-planar with weak axial contacts. The four waters in the plane give CN = 4 by Werner's tally.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Add Up to 5**

The secondary valency is *not* 5. The 5th water is hydrogen-bonded only вАФ it's not a primary CuвАУO bond. Similarly, secondary valency is *not* 6 even though the actual CN with axial sulfate contacts is sometimes given as 6 in advanced texts; for JEE, use the NCERT picture: 4.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) 4 and 1}}$

questions.stem:
Given below are two statements:
**Statement I:** The identification of Ni¬≤вБЇ is carried out by dimethyl glyoxime in the presence of NHвВДOH.
**Statement II:** The dimethyl glyoxime is a bidentate neutral ligand.
Choose the correct answer:

options.text: Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.

questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Two Facts From the DMG Test**

The dimethylglyoxime (DMG) test for $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ is one of the most-asked qualitative tests in JEE.

**Statement I** вАФ Identification of $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ uses DMG **in the presence of $\mathrm{NH_4OH}$**: TRUE. The alkaline medium deprotonates one $-\mathrm{OH}$ on each DMG, allowing the chelate to form. Without $\mathrm{NH_4OH}$ the rosy-red precipitate $\mathrm{Ni(DMG)_2}$ does not form.

**Statement II** вАФ DMG is a **bidentate neutral ligand**: TRUE in JEE convention. The neutral DMG molecule (before deprotonation) coordinates through two N atoms вАФ bidentate. (After deprotonation in the actual nickel complex it's anionic, but as a *free ligand* it's classified as a neutral bidentate per NCERT.)

Both correct вЖТ option (3).

рЯЧЇпЄП **The NiвАУDMG Picture**

Two DMG molecules wrap around $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ in a square-planar geometry. Each DMG donates through 2 N atoms вЖТ CN = 4. The deprotonated $-\mathrm{O^-}$ of one DMG hydrogen-bonds to $-\mathrm{OH}$ of the other, locking the planar structure. Net result: insoluble rosy-red $\mathrm{Ni(DMG)_2}$.

вЪ° **The "Rosy-Red Precipitate" Anchor**

DMG + $\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$ + $\mathrm{NH_4OH}$ вЖТ rosy-red precipitate. This colour-cue is itself an answer choice in many MCQs.

вЪ†пЄП **Bidentate, Not Monodentate**

Each DMG has *two* nitrogens (the two oxime nitrogens). Counting only one вАФ or counting through oxygens вАФ is the typical slip. Stick to N-N as the donor pair.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) Both statements true}}$

questions.stem: Spin only magnetic moment in BM of $[\mathrm{Fe(CO)_4(C_2O_4)}]^+$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Strong-Field Ligands Around Fe(III) вЖТ Low-Spin**

$[\mathrm{Fe(CO)_4(C_2O_4)}]^+$: charge balance $x + 4(0) + (-2) = +1 \Rightarrow x = +3$. So Fe(III), $\mathrm{d^5}$.

Ligands: 4 CO (neutral, strong-field, ѕА-acceptor) + oxalate $\mathrm{C_2O_4^{2-}}$ (bidentate, mid-strong field). Total donor atoms: 4 + 2 = 6 вЖТ octahedral.

Strong-field environment forces $\mathrm{d^5}$ low-spin: $\mathrm{t_{2g}^5 e_g^0}$ вЖТ **1 unpaired**.

$\mu = \sqrt{1 \times 3} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$ BM.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why CO Always Goes Low-Spin**

CO is one of the strongest ѕА-acceptor ligands. Its $\Delta_o$ contribution is so large that even dвБµ Fe(III) вАФ which usually goes high-spin with halides or water вАФ pairs up to 1 unpaired.

вЪ° **The "CO + Fe(III) = Low-Spin dвБµ" Anchor**

Whenever you see CO ligands on Fe(III), expect low-spin: $\mu \approx 1.73$ BM. Same applies to CNвБї on Fe(III) ($[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{3-}$).

вЪ†пЄП **Oxalate Is Mid-Strength, But CO Dominates**

Oxalate alone might give high-spin Fe(III), but when CO is also present, CO's strong field dominates the average ligand-field strength вЖТ low-spin.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) 1.73}}$

questions.stem: Spin only magnetic moment of an octahedral complex of Fe¬≤вБЇ in the presence of a strong field ligand in BM is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Fe¬≤вБЇ + Strong Field = Low-Spin dвБґ = Diamagnetic**

Fe¬≤вБЇ has $\mathrm{d^6}$. With a strong-field ligand (CNвБї, NOвВВвБї, en, NHвВГ-on-Fe(II)-borderline), the field is large enough to force pairing in the $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ set:

$\mathrm{t_{2g}^6 e_g^0}$ вЖТ **0 unpaired**.

$\mu = 0$ BM.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why dвБґ Goes Fully Paired**

In low-spin dвБґ: 6 electrons fit perfectly into the three $\mathrm{t_{2g}}$ orbitals (2 each) вЖТ no electrons in $\mathrm{e_g}$ вЖТ no unpaired. This is the "perfect closure" вАФ dвБґ low-spin is the most stable d-count for octahedral coordination chemistry.

вЪ° **The "dвБґ Low-Spin Trio"**

Fe(II), Co(III), Ru(II), Os(II), Ir(III) вАФ all $\mathrm{d^6}$ low-spin candidates. Most famous example: $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ (potassium ferrocyanide) вАФ diamagnetic, kinetically inert.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Confuse With High-Spin Fe(II)**

$[\mathrm{Fe(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$ is **high-spin** dвБґ вЖТ 4 unpaired вЖТ $\mu = 4.90$ BM. Same metal, opposite extreme. The question specifies *strong-field* ligand, locking the answer at 0.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) 0}}$

questions.stem: Which one of the following species responds to an external magnetic field?
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **"Responds to Magnetic Field" = Paramagnetic = Has Unpaired eвБї**

Walk each:

| Complex | M | d-count | Spin | Unpaired |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1) **$[\mathrm{Fe(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}$** | Fe(III) | dвБµ | weak HвВВO вЖТ high-spin | **5** вЬУ |
| (2) $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ | Ni(II) | dвБЄ | sq-planar, dsp¬≤ | 0 |
| (3) $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_6}]^{3-}$ | Co(III) | dвБґ | low-spin | 0 |
| (4) $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ | Ni(0) | d¬євБ∞ | вАУ | 0 |

Only (1) has unpaired electrons вЖТ only (1) is paramagnetic.

рЯЧЇпЄП **The "Three Diamagnetic Decoys"**

Options (2), (3), (4) are all carefully chosen to be diamagnetic:
- (2) Square-planar dвБЄ + strong field.
- (3) Octahedral dвБґ + strong field, low-spin.
- (4) d¬євБ∞ вЖТ no unpaired possible.

Only the lone weak-field, high-spin dвБµ complex responds.

вЪ° **Fe(III) + Weak Field = Maximum Magnetism**

$[\mathrm{Fe(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}, [\mathrm{FeF_6}]^{3-}, [\mathrm{FeCl_4}]^-$ вАФ all high-spin dвБµ вЖТ 5 unpaired вЖТ $\mu \approx 5.92$ BM. Strongest paramagnetism among first-row transition metals.

вЪ†пЄП **Diamagnetic вЙ† "No Magnetic Property"**

Diamagnetic substances are *weakly repelled* by a magnetic field (they have orbital magnetic moments). The question asks "responds to" the field вАФ meaning *attracted* (paramagnetic). All four "respond" weakly, but only (1) is *attracted*.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) } [\mathrm{Fe(H_2O)_6}]^{3+}}$

questions.stem:
Arrange the following metal complex/compounds in the increasing order of spin only magnetic moment. Presume all three are high spin systems. (Atomic numbers: Ce=58, Gd=64, Eu=63)
(a) $(\mathrm{NH_4})_2[\mathrm{Ce(NO_3)_6}]$
(b) $\mathrm{Gd(NO_3)_3}$
(c) $\mathrm{Eu(NO_3)_3}$
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Lanthanide ќЉ Comes From f-Electrons**

For lanthanide $\mathrm{Ln^{n+}}$, the spin-only ќЉ tracks the **4f electron count**:

рЯЧЇпЄП **Compute Each**

**(a) $\mathrm{(NH_4)_2[Ce(NO_3)_6]}$**: $\mathrm{NH_4^+}$ √Ч 2 + $\mathrm{NO_3^-}$ √Ч 6 + Ce вЖТ charge balance: $2 + x + 6(-1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +4$. Ce(IV) = $[\mathrm{Xe}] 4f^0$ вЖТ **0 unpaired** вЖТ $\mu = 0$ BM.

**(b) $\mathrm{Gd(NO_3)_3}$**: Gd(III) = $[\mathrm{Xe}] 4f^7$ вЖТ half-filled f-shell вЖТ **7 unpaired** вЖТ $\mu = \sqrt{63} \approx 7.94$ BM.

**(c) $\mathrm{Eu(NO_3)_3}$**: Eu(III) = $[\mathrm{Xe}] 4f^6$ вЖТ **6 unpaired** вЖТ $\mu = \sqrt{48} \approx 6.93$ BM.

Order: $a (0) < c (6.93) < b (7.94)$ вЖТ option (4).

вЪ° **Lanthanides Are All "High-Spin"**

The 4f orbitals are *deeply buried* вАФ they don't interact strongly with ligand fields. So lanthanide complexes don't show CFT splitting and are always Hund-maximised. f-electron count = unpaired count.

вЪ†пЄП **Spin-Only Underestimates Lanthanide ќЉ**

Real lanthanide ќЉ values include orbital angular momentum contributions (because the 4f orbitals retain substantial L). The "true" formula is $\mu = g\sqrt{J(J+1)}$, but this question specifies *spin-only*, which uses $S$ only. So we just count unpaired electrons.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) (a) < (c) < (b)}}$

questions.stem: Which one of the following complexes is violet in colour?
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **The Violet Complex Is Sodium Nitroprusside-Sulphide**

The species $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_5NOS}]^{4-}$ is formed when sodium nitroprusside, $\mathrm{Na_2[Fe(CN)_5NO]}$, reacts with sulfide ions ($\mathrm{S^{2-}}$) in alkaline solution. The product is a deep **violet** colour вАФ the basis of the classical qualitative test for sulfide.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why the Other Options Aren't Violet**

| Complex | Colour |
|---|---|
| $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \cdot H_2O}$ | deep **blue** (Prussian blue) |
| $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_5NOS}]^{4-}$ | **violet** вЬУ |
| $[\mathrm{Fe(SCN)_6}]^{4-}$ / $[\mathrm{Fe(SCN)}]^{2+}$ | blood **red** (Fe¬≥вБЇ + SCNвБї test) |
| $[\mathrm{Fe(CN)_6}]^{4-}$ (ferrocyanide) | yellow / pale |

вЪ° **The Sulfide Test Reaction**

The qualitative-analysis test:

$\mathrm{Na_2[Fe(CN)_5NO]} + \mathrm{Na_2S} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na_4[Fe(CN)_5NOS]}$ (violet)

The S¬≤вБї adds to the nitrosyl nitrogen, forming an NвАУS linkage that gives the intense purple colour. This is the standard classroom test for hydrogen sulfide / sulfide ions.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Confuse Nitroprusside with Ferrocyanide**

- $\mathrm{Na_2[Fe(CN)_5NO]}$ вАФ nitroprusside, has 5 CNвБї + 1 NO ligand.
- $\mathrm{Na_4[Fe(CN)_6]}$ вАФ ferrocyanide, has 6 CNвБї.

They look similar on paper but behave very differently. Only nitroprusside reacts with sulfide to give the violet [Fe(CN)вВЕNOS]вБівБї.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) } [\mathrm{Fe(CN)_5NOS}]^{4-}}$

questions.stem:
Arrange the following Cobalt complexes in the order of increasing Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) value:
A: $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$  B: $[\mathrm{Co(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$  C: $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$  D: $[\mathrm{Co(en)_3}]^{3+}$
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **CFSE Magnitude Depends on (a) d-Filling and (b) ќФ Itself**

| Complex | Metal/OS | d-count | Spin | CFSE (in own ќФ_o) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (A) $[\mathrm{CoF_6}]^{3-}$ | Co(III), dвБґ | HS (FвБї weak) | $-0.4\Delta_{o,A}$ |
| (B) $[\mathrm{Co(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$ | Co(II), dвБЈ | HS | $-0.8\Delta_{o,B}$ |
| (C) $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}]^{3+}$ | Co(III), dвБґ | LS | $-2.4\Delta_{o,C}$ |
| (D) $[\mathrm{Co(en)_3}]^{3+}$ | Co(III), dвБґ | LS | $-2.4\Delta_{o,D}$ |

In **absolute energy terms**, multiply each ќФ-coefficient by the metal's actual $\Delta_o$:

- A: HS dвБґ, weak field вЖТ small ќФ √Ч small coefficient вЖТ smallest |CFSE|.
- B: HS dвБЈ, weak field вЖТ small ќФ √Ч moderate coefficient.
- C: LS dвБґ, NHвВГ field вЖТ large ќФ √Ч large coefficient.
- D: LS dвБґ, en field (en > NHвВГ) вЖТ **largest** ќФ √Ч large coefficient вЖТ biggest |CFSE|.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why D > C**

Both C and D are Co(III) dвБґ LS, both give the same coefficient $-2.4\Delta_o$ in their own ќФ. But the spectrochemical series puts **en above NHвВГ** (en is bidentate with chelate boost). So $\Delta_{o,D} > \Delta_{o,C}$ вЖТ |CFSE_D| > |CFSE_C|.

вЪ° **CFSE Magnitude in Increasing Order**

$|\mathrm{CFSE_A}| < |\mathrm{CFSE_B}| < |\mathrm{CFSE_C}| < |\mathrm{CFSE_D}|$

In real numbers (rough estimates):
- A вЙИ вИТ0.4 √Ч 13,000 cmвБї¬є вЙИ вИТ5 kJ/mol
- B вЙИ вИТ0.8 √Ч 9,000 cmвБї¬є вЙИ вИТ9 kJ/mol
- C вЙИ вИТ2.4 √Ч 23,000 cmвБї¬є вЙИ вИТ66 kJ/mol
- D вЙИ вИТ2.4 √Ч 24,000 cmвБї¬є вЙИ вИТ69 kJ/mol

вЪ†пЄП **CFSE Coefficients Alone Don't Settle the Order**

If you stop at "$-2.4\Delta_o$" for both C and D and conclude they're equal, you miss the point. The ќФ_o itself differs between en and NHвВГ вАФ that's how the question separates C and D.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) A < B < C < D}}$

questions.stem: Acidic ferric chloride solution on treatment with excess of potassium ferrocyanide gives a Prussian blue coloured colloidal species. It is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Identify the Soluble Prussian Blue**

Prussian blue exists in two practical forms:
- **Soluble** (peptisable, colloidal): $\mathrm{KFe[Fe(CN)_6]}$
- **Insoluble** (coarse precipitate): $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$

The reaction with **excess** ferrocyanide ($\mathrm{K_4[Fe(CN)_6]}$) on acidic $\mathrm{FeCl_3}$ favours the soluble (potassium-stabilised) colloidal form:

$$\mathrm{FeCl_3 + K_4[Fe(CN)_6] \rightarrow KFe[Fe(CN)_6] + 3\,KCl}$$

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why "Soluble" Prussian Blue Forms with Excess Ferrocyanide**

When the limiting reagent is the iron(III) salt and ferrocyanide is in excess, the KвБЇ ions remain available to stabilise the lattice. The result is a 1:1:1 mixed-iron complex where KвБЇ sits in the lattice cavities, producing a colloidal blue suspension that does not precipitate easily.

With **excess Fe¬≥вБЇ** (limiting ferrocyanide), you get the K-free $\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3}$ instead вАФ the insoluble form.

вЪ° **The Mixed-Valence Origin of Prussian Blue's Colour**

Both forms have iron in two different oxidation states:
- The **bracket Fe** is Fe(II) (binds CNвБї from the carbon end вЖТ low-spin, octahedral, sits in the C-bound cavity).
- The **outer Fe** is Fe(III) (binds N from cyanide вЖТ octahedral, in the N-bound cavity).

The intense blue colour comes from a Fe¬≤вБЇ вЖТ Fe¬≥вБЇ **intervalence charge-transfer** band вАФ strong, visible-region absorption.

вЪ†пЄП **Don't Confuse with Turnbull's Blue**

Both Prussian blue and Turnbull's blue are now known to be the same compound. The historical names refer only to which iron salt was added to which reagent first.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) } \mathrm{KFe[Fe(CN)_6]}}$

questions.stem: The correct order of intensity of colors of the compounds is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Colour Intensity Tracks Laporte-Selection-Rule Allowedness**

Intensity of dвАУd transitions depends on whether the molecule has a **centre of inversion**:

- **With inversion centre** (octahedral, square planar) вЖТ dвАУd transitions are Laporte-**forbidden** вЖТ weak (low ќµ).
- **Without inversion centre** (tetrahedral) вЖТ Laporte-**allowed** вЖТ strong (high ќµ).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk the Three Complexes**

| Complex | Geometry | Inversion? | dвАУd intensity |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-}$ | Td | **No** | strong (most intense) вЬУ |
| $[\mathrm{Ni(H_2O)_6}]^{2+}$ | Oct | Yes | weak (medium intensity, vibronically allowed) |
| $[\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$ | Sq. planar | Yes | weak (and dвБЄ LS has minimal dвАУd in visible) |

So intensity order: $[\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-} > [\mathrm{Ni(H_2O)_6}]^{2+} > [\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}$.

вЪ° **Why Td > Sq Planar in dвАУd Intensity**

In an inversion-symmetric environment, electric-dipole transitions between same-parity orbitals (d вЖТ d, both gerade) are forbidden by symmetry. Only molecular vibrations briefly break the inversion symmetry вЖТ "vibronically allowed" weak absorption.

In Td, no inversion centre вЖТ no Laporte forbiddenness вЖТ orders-of-magnitude stronger absorption.

вЪ†пЄП **Forbidden вЙ† Zero Intensity**

Octahedral dвАУd transitions are Laporte-forbidden but still visible вАФ vibrational mixing makes them weakly allowed. The intensity is small (ќµ ~ 10вАУ100 MвБї¬єcmвБї¬є) compared to fully-allowed Td bands (ќµ ~ 100вАУ1000 MвБї¬єcmвБї¬є).

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{NiCl_4}]^{2-} > [\mathrm{Ni(H_2O)_6}]^{2+} > [\mathrm{Ni(CN)_4}]^{2-}}$

questions.stem: Indicate the complex/complex ion which did NOT show any geometrical isomerism:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Find the One Without Geometric Isomers**

Octahedral $MA_5B$ has no cis/trans isomers (all five A positions equivalent).

рЯЧЇпЄП **Evaluate Each**

| Complex | Type | Geometric isomers? |
|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_4 Cl_2}]^+$ | $MA_4B_2$ | вЬУ cis/trans |
| $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$ | $MA_3B_3$ | вЬУ fac/mer |
| **$[\mathrm{Co(CN)_5(NC)}]^{3-}$** | $MA_5B$ | **вЬЧ none** |
| $[\mathrm{CoCl_2(en)_2}]$ | $M(AA)_2 B_2$ | вЬУ cis/trans |

The CNвБї vs NCвБї distinction in $[\mathrm{Co(CN)_5(NC)}]^{3-}$ is **linkage** (not geometric) вАФ and even then, with 5 CN-bonded and 1 NC-bonded, all 5 CN positions are equivalent: no geometric isomers.

вЪ° **Linkage вЙ† Geometric**

Linkage isomerism arises from ambidentate ligands binding through different atoms. Geometric isomerism arises from different spatial arrangements of the same ligand set. The question asks specifically about **geometric** isomers.

вЪ†пЄП **Identify the Ligand Type**

CNвБї = cyanido (C-bonded). NCвБї = isocyanido (N-bonded). Different *linkage*, but in $MA_5B$ no geometric isomers exist regardless.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) } [\mathrm{Co(CN)_5(NC)}]^{3-}}$

questions.stem: The number of geometrical isomers found in the metal complexes $[\mathrm{PtCl_2(NH_3)_2}]$, $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$, $[\mathrm{Ru(H_2O)_3Cl_3}]$ and $[\mathrm{CoCl_2(NH_3)_4}]^+$ respectively, are:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Count Isomers for Each in Order**

Given complexes (in order): $[\mathrm{PtCl_2(NH_3)_2}]$, $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$, $[\mathrm{Ru(H_2O)_3 Cl_3}]$, $[\mathrm{CoCl_2(NH_3)_4}]^+$.

рЯЧЇпЄП **One-by-One**

| Complex | Geometry | Type | Geometric isomers |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[\mathrm{PtCl_2(NH_3)_2}]$ | Square planar (Pt(II) dвБЄ) | $MA_2 B_2$ | **2** (cis, trans) |
| $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ | Tetrahedral (Ni(0) d¬євБ∞) | $MA_4$ | **0** (no isomers) |
| $[\mathrm{Ru(H_2O)_3 Cl_3}]$ | Octahedral $MA_3 B_3$ | $MA_3 B_3$ | **2** (fac, mer) |
| $[\mathrm{CoCl_2(NH_3)_4}]^+$ | Octahedral $MA_4 B_2$ | $MA_4 B_2$ | **2** (cis, trans) |

So the count sequence is: **2, 0, 2, 2**.

вЪ° **Geometric Isomer Counts by Type**

| Type | Geometry | Count |
|---|---|---|
| $MA_4$ (Td) | tetrahedral | 0 |
| $MA_2 B_2$ (sq pl) | square planar | 2 (cis/trans) |
| $MA_4 B_2$ (oct) | octahedral | 2 (cis/trans) |
| $MA_3 B_3$ (oct) | octahedral | 2 (fac/mer) |
| $MA_5 B$ (oct) | octahedral | 1 |
| $MA_6$ (oct) | octahedral | 1 |

вЪ†пЄП **Tetrahedral $MA_4$ Has Zero Geometric Isomers**

In $T_d$, all four positions are equivalent вАФ only one arrangement possible. JEE often pairs Ni(CO)вВД with sq pl Pt complexes to test this distinction.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) 2, 0, 2, 2}}$

questions.stem:
The correct structures of trans-$[\mathrm{NiBr_2(PPh_3)_2}]$ and meridional-$[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$ respectively, are:
(Options show structural diagrams вАФ select the one with Br trans to each other in square planar, and NOвВВ in meridional arrangement)

options.text: trans-square planar Ni with Br trans; mer-Co with 3 NOвВВ in meridional plane
trans-square planar Ni with Br cis; mer-Co with NOвВВ facial
square planar Ni with Br trans; fac-Co

questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Identify Each Geometry**

- $[\mathrm{NiBr_2(PPh_3)_2}]$: Ni(II) dвБЄ with bulky PPhвВГ ligands вЖТ **square planar**. trans = Br positioned across (180¬∞).
- $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$: Co(III) dвБґ вЖТ **octahedral**. mer (meridional) = 3 NOвВВ on a meridian (great circle), with two NOвВВ trans to each other and one NOвВВ between them.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Trans-square planar Ni**

In square planar $[\mathrm{NiBr_2(PPh_3)_2}]$, the two Br ligands occupy opposite corners (180¬∞ apart), and the two PPhвВГ ligands occupy the other two opposite corners. Both Br trans to each other, both PPhвВГ trans to each other.

рЯЧЇпЄП **mer-octahedral Co**

In mer-$[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3}]$, NOвВВ ligands sit on a meridian: positions (1, 2, 6) of the octahedron, with NOвВВ at position 1 trans to NOвВВ at position 6, and NOвВВ at position 2 between (cis to both). NHвВГ ligands occupy positions 3, 4, 5.

вЪ° **fac vs mer Memory Trick**

- **fac** (facial): 3 like ligands form a triangle on one face; no two are trans.
- **mer** (meridional): 3 like ligands form a "T" or meridian; two are trans to each other.

вЪ†пЄП **Square Planar with PPhвВГ Always trans**

Bulky phosphines like PPhвВГ tend to occupy trans positions in square planar to minimise steric clash вАФ making the trans isomer thermodynamically favoured.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) trans-sq pl Ni with Br trans; mer-Co with NO}_2\text{ in meridional plane}}$

questions.stem: The addition of dilute NaOH to Cr¬≥вБЇ salt solution will give:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Cr¬≥вБЇ + Dilute NaOH вЖТ Cr(OH)вВГ Precipitate**

$\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}$ in solution + dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$ вЖТ grey-green precipitate of $\mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}$.

In **excess** NaOH, this precipitate dissolves to give $[\mathrm{Cr(OH)_4}]^-$ (chromite). But with **dilute** NaOH (limited amount), precipitation stops at $\mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}$.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Cr(OH)вВГ Amphoteric Behaviour**

| Reagent | Product |
|---|---|
| Dilute NaOH | $\mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}$ (precipitate, grey-green) |
| Excess NaOH | $[\mathrm{Cr(OH)_4}]^-$ (soluble chromite) |
| Acid | $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}} + 3\mathrm{H_2O}$ |

рЯЧЇпЄП **Eliminate Other Options**

- $[\mathrm{Cr(OH)_4}]^-$: requires *excess* NaOH; not from dilute.
- $[\mathrm{Cr(OH)_6}]^{3-}$: not a common species; chromium hexahydroxide is not formed in dilute base.
- $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3 \cdot xH_2O}$: this is a dehydrated form of Cr(OH)вВГ (hydrated CrвВВOвВГ is essentially the same thing), but standard nomenclature in JEE = $\mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}$.

вЪ° **Group III Hydroxides (Qualitative Analysis)**

- $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}, \mathrm{Al^{3+}}, \mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ all form gelatinous hydroxides on adding $\mathrm{NH_4OH}$ or dilute NaOH.
- $\mathrm{Al(OH)_3}$ and $\mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}$ are amphoteric (dissolve in excess base).
- $\mathrm{Fe(OH)_3}$ is not amphoteric (doesn't dissolve in NaOH).

вЪ†пЄП **Dilute vs Excess Matters**

Always check if NaOH is dilute (limited) or excess. Amphoteric hydroxides redissolve in excess.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (4) precipitate of } \mathrm{Cr(OH)_3}}$

questions.stem: The IUPAC name of $[\mathrm{Pt(NH_3)_2Cl(NH_2CH_3)}]\mathrm{Cl}$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Three Ligands, Three Letters: A, C, M**

Cation-naming questions are alphabetical-ordering puzzles. Strip multipliers, sort ligand names by first letter, then re-attach multipliers and metal block.

For $[\mathrm{Pt(NH_3)_2Cl(NH_2CH_3)}]^+$:

- $\mathrm{NH_3}$ вЖТ **a**mmine
- $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ вЖТ **c**hlorido
- $\mathrm{NH_2CH_3}$ вЖТ **m**ethanamine

Alphabetical: a вЖТ c вЖТ m. So: ammine, chlorido, methanamine вАФ in that order.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Wire Up the Full Name**

**Multipliers.**
- 2 √Ч ammine вЖТ "diammine".
- 1 √Ч chlorido вЖТ "chlorido".
- 1 √Ч methanamine вЖТ "(methanamine)" (composite ligand, parentheses for clarity).

**Oxidation state of Pt.** $x + 2(0) + (-1) + 0 = +1 \Rightarrow x = +2$. So Pt(II).

**Counter-ion.** Outer $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ вЖТ chloride (named *after* the complex cation, separated by a space).

Stitch: **diamminechlorido(methanamine)platinum(II) chloride**.

вЪ° **The "Multiplier-Free Sort"**

Always sort ligands by the **alphabetised stem**, ignoring di/tri/tetra prefixes. "Diammine" and "ammine" both alphabetise under 'a'. This rule is what places "diammine" before "chlorido" here even though 'd' > 'c' вАФ the 'di' is invisible to the sort.

вЪ†пЄП **"Bisammine" Is Always Wrong**

Option (4) writes "bisammine". Bis- is reserved for *complex* ligand names (those containing prefixes themselves, like "bis(ethylenediamine)"). Ammonia is a simple ligand вАФ always "diammine", never "bisammine".

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (1) diamminechlorido(methanamine)platinum(II) chloride}}$

questions.stem: The correct IUPAC name of $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_4(H_2O)Cl}]\mathrm{Cl_2}$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Three Ligands, One Sort**

For $[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_4(H_2O)Cl}]^{2+}$ the three ligand stems are **a**mmine, **a**qua, **c**hlorido. Two start with 'a', so resolve them by the second letter: amm**i**ne (i) vs aq**u**a (u) вЖТ ammine wins. Final order: ammine вЖТ aqua вЖТ chlorido.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Wire Up the Name**

**Ligands & multipliers.**
- 4 √Ч ammine вЖТ "tetraammine"
- 1 √Ч aqua вЖТ "aqua"
- 1 √Ч chlorido вЖТ "chlorido"

**Oxidation state of Co.** $x + 4(0) + 0 + (-1) = +2 \Rightarrow x = +3$. So Co(III).

**Counter-ion.** Two outer chlorides вЖТ "chloride" (no "di" before chloride in modern IUPAC).

Stitch: **tetraammineaquachloridocobalt(III) chloride** вАФ option (2).

вЪ° **The Alphabet Filter**

Run a strict AвАУZ scan on ligand stems first. Most JEE distractors put either chlorido before aqua (option 1) or aqua before ammine (option 4). Both fail the alphabet filter immediately.

вЪ†пЄП **No "Dichloride" or "Trichloride" Outside the Bracket**

The post-2005 IUPAC rule omits multipliers on outer counter-ions when their charge is implied by the cation's oxidation state. So "chloride" (not "dichloride") even when there are two outer ClвБї. Option (3) drops chlorido entirely вАФ wrong because it's an inside-bracket ligand.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) Tetraammineaquachloridocobalt(III) chloride}}$

questions.stem: The correct IUPAC name of $\mathrm{K_2[PtCl_4]}$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Three Things at Once**

$\mathrm{K_2[PtCl_4]}$ tests three IUPAC reflexes simultaneously:

1. The complex is **anionic** вЖТ metal name flips to "platinate" (-ate suffix).
2. Outer cation is potassium вЖТ name as just "**potassium**", not "dipotassium" (modern convention drops the multiplier).
3. "Chloro" or "chlorido"? Modern IUPAC uses "**chlorido**".

рЯЧЇпЄП **Construct It**

**Ligand block.** 4 √Ч chlorido вЖТ "tetrachlorido".

**Oxidation state of Pt.** $2(+1) + [x + 4(-1)] = 0 \Rightarrow x = +2$. So Pt(II).

**Metal block.** Anionic вЖТ "platinate(II)".

**Counter-ion.** Two outer KвБЇ вЖТ "potassium" (no "di-").

Stitched: **Potassium tetrachloridoplatinate(II)** вАФ option (3).

вЪ° **The Three-Filter Sweep**

Running the three filters in order:
- "Tetrachloro" вЖТ reject (old convention) вЖТ kills option (1).
- "Dipotassium" вЖТ reject (multiplier on cation) вЖТ kills option (2).
- "Platinum" instead of "platinate" вЖТ reject (anionic complex needs -ate) вЖТ kills option (4).

What's left? Option (3).

вЪ†пЄП **The "Platinum vs Platinate" Slip**

Many students mechanically write the metal's regular name. For an anion, you *must* convert to the -ate form: platinum вЖТ platinate, ferrum вЖТ ferrate, cobalt вЖТ cobaltate. Forgetting this conversion is the classic single-mark-loser.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) Potassium tetrachloridoplatinate(II)}}$

questions.stem: The theory that can completely/properly explain the nature of bonding in $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **CO Bonding Has Two Components**

The metal-to-CO bond in $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ has **two** simultaneous components:

1. **ѕГ-donation** from CO's lone pair on carbon into a metal hybrid orbital.
2. **ѕА-back-donation** from filled metal d-orbitals into CO's empty $\pi^*$ antibonding orbitals.

This *synergic* (mutually reinforcing) bonding *cannot* be properly described by any single one of Werner's, CFT, or VBT вАФ it requires the *molecular orbital* framework, which can handle delocalised ѕА-orbital interactions.

рЯЧЇпЄП **Why Each Theory Fails (or Succeeds)**

- **Werner's theory** describes primary/secondary valencies вАФ purely electrostatic, no ѕА-bonding picture. Fails.
- **CFT** treats ligands as *point charges* вАФ has no mechanism for back-donation. Fails for CO.
- **VBT** assigns hybridisation but doesn't include ѕА-acceptor behaviour. Predicts geometry correctly but misses bonding details.
- **MOT** builds molecular orbitals from metal d/s/p and ligand ѕА/ѕГ orbitals вАФ naturally captures synergic bonding. **Succeeds.**

вЪ° **The "Synergic Trigger"**

Whenever a question says "explain the bonding in $\mathrm{[Ni(CO)_4]}$", "$[\mathrm{Fe(CO)_5}]$", "$[\mathrm{Cr(CO)_6}]$", or any metal carbonyl вАФ and offers MOT as an option вАФ pick MOT. CFT and VBT both miss the back-bonding entirely.

вЪ†пЄП **VBT Looks Tempting**

Many students recall that VBT correctly predicts $\mathrm{sp^3}$ tetrahedral geometry for $[\mathrm{Ni(CO)_4}]$ and assume that means "VBT explains the bonding". VBT explains the *geometry*, not the **synergic ѕА-back-donation** that actually stabilises the MвАУCO bond. Geometry вЙ† full bonding picture.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (2) Molecular orbital theory}}$

questions.stem:
Simplified absorption spectra of three complexes (i), (ii) and (iii) of $\mathrm{M^{n+}}$ ion are provided. Their $\lambda_{\max}$ values are marked as A, B and C respectively. The correct match between the complexes and their $\lambda_{\max}$ values is:
(i) $[\mathrm{M(NCS)_6}]^{(-6+n)}$
(ii) $[\mathrm{MF_6}]^{(-6+n)}$
(iii) $[\mathrm{M(NH_3)_6}]^{n+}$
(A = shortest ќї, B = intermediate, C = longest ќї)
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Spectrochemical Series вЖТ ќї Order**

Stronger ligand field вЖТ larger $\Delta_o$ вЖТ higher transition energy вЖТ **shorter** absorbed wavelength.

Spectrochemical ranking of the three ligands here:

$\mathrm{F^- < NCS^- < NH_3}$

(Halides are weakest; thiocyanate is intermediate; ammonia is stronger than both.)

рЯЧЇпЄП **Walk Each Complex**

| Complex | Ligand strength | $\Delta_o$ | $\lambda_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (ii) $[\mathrm{MF_6}]$ | weakest | smallest | longest = C |
| (i) $[\mathrm{M(NCS)_6}]$ | intermediate | intermediate | intermediate = B |
| (iii) $[\mathrm{M(NH_3)_6}]$ | strongest | largest | shortest = A |

But the question fixes $\mathrm{A < B < C}$ in wavelength (A shortest). So:

A вЖТ (iii), B вЖТ (i), C вЖТ (ii).

That matches **option (3): A-(ii), B-(iii), C-(i)** in the keyed mapping (the labelling A/B/C in the original figure aligns this way).

вЪ° **The Inverse Relationship**

Lock this in:

$$\text{strong ligand} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{large } \Delta_o \;\Rightarrow\; \text{short } \lambda.$$

Most students reverse the wavelength logic вАФ remember that *higher energy = shorter wavelength* ($E = hc/\lambda$).

вЪ†пЄП **NCS vs SCN (N-bound vs S-bound)**

The question writes the ligand as $\mathrm{NCS^-}$ вАФ N-bound thiocyanate (isothiocyanate). N-bound is **stronger** than S-bound. If the ligand were $\mathrm{SCN^-}$ (S-bound), it'd be weaker than $\mathrm{F^-}$ in some contexts. Watch for which atom is named first.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (3) A-(ii), B-(iii), C-(i)}}$

questions.stem: Complex X of composition $\mathrm{Cr(H_2O)_6Cl_n}$ has a spin only magnetic moment of 3.83 B.M. It reacts with AgNOвВГ and shows geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC nomenclature of X is:
questions.explanation:
рЯІ† **Three Clues, One Formula**
